patterns.name: Stopping Too Early
patterns.what: Tends to explain the background science well but occasionally forgets to state the final consequence or observation.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 correctly explained that smaller pieces increase surface area, but stopped short of stating that this makes digestion faster.
AL4 knew the seed leaf provides food, but forgot to explicitly state the final observation that its mass decreases.

patterns.strategic_advice: When you see **'explain why'** or **'explain your answer'**, make sure to include a **'so that...'** or **'therefore...'** clause to show the final result.

patterns.name: Air Volume Confusion
patterns.what: Sometimes mixes up the properties of air and solids, particularly forgetting that air has no definite shape and will expand to fill any empty space.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 thought that both air and a pencil have a definite shape, forgetting that air takes the shape of its container.
AL4 missed that when water flows out of a container, air immediately rushes in to fill the newly emptied space.

patterns.strategic_advice: When dealing with containers, remember that **air is sneaky**! It doesn't have a fixed shape or volume, so it will always stretch out to fill whatever **empty space** is left behind by water or solids.

patterns.name: Missing Experiment Steps
patterns.what: Occasionally gives a very short answer or leaves the question blank when asked to describe the exact steps of a science experiment.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Instead of explaining how to move the paper clip to test the magnet's strength, AL4 just wrote that a ruler was used.
AL4 left the question blank when asked to explain how to test if a metal bar is a magnet.
AL4 skipped the question asking to describe how to make a temporary magnet stronger using the stroking method.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When a question asks you to **'describe how'** or **'explain how'** an experiment works, imagine you are writing an instruction manual for a friend.
- **List the exact actions** (e.g., 'move the paper clip', 'bring both ends close').
- **State what to measure or observe** to get the result.

patterns.name: Flowchart Path Slips
patterns.what: Sometimes takes a wrong turn on a classification flowchart by misreading one of the yes/no questions about an organism's traits.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: AL4 followed the wrong path for a plant, perhaps forgetting that plants can move their parts on their own to face the sun.
AL4 picked the wrong animal classification, likely by mixing up which traits belong to mammals versus fishes.

patterns.strategic_advice: When tackling a flowchart, trace the path with your pencil and double-check each **'Yes' or 'No'** decision. If you get stuck on a tricky question like 'Does it move?', remember that **all living things move**, even plants!
